name: Ladenia — Greek Tomato & Onion Pizza (no cheese)
description: Ladenia is the traditional cheeseless 'pizza' of the Aegean islands Kimolos and Milos — a focaccia-style dough baked in a well-oiled pan under thinly sliced tomatoes and onion with oregano and good olive oil. Naturally low/no cheese (feta is an optional modern add). From The Mediterranean Dish.
recipeIngredient: 1/4 cup extra-virgin olive oil, plus more for greasing the pan (be generous)
1 tablespoon dried oregano, ground in a mortar/spice grinder
1/2 cup Kalamata olives, pitted and sliced (optional)
Feta — omit for the traditional no-cheese version (or a light crumble if desired)

recipeInstructions.text: Make the dough: whisk the yeast, sugar, and lukewarm water; let sit until bubbly, ~5 minutes. Add the flour, 1/4 cup olive oil, and salt; mix to a soft, pliable dough. Adjust with a little more water or flour as needed.
Generously oil a 12-inch cast-iron skillet or round cake pan (oil about 1 inch deep up the sides). Transfer the dough in and press, jiggle, and stretch it toward the edges like focaccia.
Cover with a damp towel and proof 30 minutes (or up to ~1 hour for a more open crumb).
Make the topping: toss the sliced tomatoes, onion, oregano, 1/2 cup olive oil, and olives with a pinch of salt — gently, so the tomatoes stay intact.
Preheat oven to 400°F. Re-stretch and dimple the proofed dough with your fingertips.
Spread the tomato-onion mixture evenly over the dough, leaving a small border; spoon the accumulated juices and oil over the top. (Add a light feta crumble only if you want it.)
Bake 30–40 minutes, until the onions caramelize on top and the base is browned and cooked through. Serve warm or at room temperature.

author: The Mediterranean Dish

ai.cliffNotes: An olive-oil-rich Greek island flatbread 'pizza' — focaccia-style dough baked in a well-oiled pan under thin tomatoes and onion with oregano. Cheeseless by tradition, so it's the right low-cheese pick for pizza night.
keyTips: The name comes from 'ladi' (oil) — olive oil is the point. Be generous oiling the pan; it shallow-fries the bottom crust crisp and golden, which is the signature of a good ladenia.
Bake lower and slower than a Neapolitan pizza (400°F, 30–40 min). It's a deep, focaccia-style base, so it needs the time to cook through under the wet topping.

Slice the tomatoes and onion thin so they soften and the onion caramelizes in the bake; toss gently so the tomatoes don't break, and pour the released tomato-oil juices over the top before baking for flavor.
Skip the feta for the genuine cheeseless version (it's optional even in this recipe) — that's exactly what makes it the low-cheese pizza you want.
It's just as good at room temperature as hot, so it's a great make-ahead; bake it before the other pizzas and let it rest while you fire those off.
